subject: The collapse of FTX and the fraud trial of Sam Bankman-Fried (2022 collapse, 2023 conviction) — where ~$8 billion of customers' crypto deposits actually went
central_question: How did a 30-year-old in shorts and a T-shirt become crypto's most trusted golden boy — and where did roughly $8 billion of his customers' money actually go?
viewer_promise: By the end you'll understand how the FTX crypto exchange really worked, the hidden software 'backdoor' that let his own trading firm Alameda spend customer deposits, how that money funded risky bets, luxury real estate and political donations, how the whole thing unraveled in a single week in November 2022, and how a federal jury in New York convicted Sam Bankman-Fried on all seven counts of fraud and conspiracy.
surprise: The money wasn't simply 'lost in a crypto crash.' Prosecutors showed FTX's code had a special exemption that let Bankman-Fried's private trading firm draw down customer deposits almost without limit — so when customers all asked for their money back at once, it was already gone. The 'effective altruist' who promised to give his fortune away was convicted of one of the largest financial frauds in US history.
stakes: Whether 'crypto' meant the rules of fraud no longer applied — and the answer the trial gave: taking customers' money and spending it as your own is still theft, no matter how new the technology or how good the cause sounds. Millions of ordinary depositors learned that an exchange holding your coins can be a black box.
target_audience: English-speaking general audience interested in true crime, money/finance, tech scandals, and how systems really work — broader and younger-skewing than pure law-history viewers
series_thread_from_prev: Miranda, Gideon and Mapp were about your rights when the state comes after you. This one flips the camera: what happens when an ordinary person's money is taken — and the US courts are what finally hold the powerful to account. Same channel question — how the system really works — applied to modern financial fraud.
demand_evidence: FTX, Sam Bankman-Fried and 'crypto scam' are extremely high-recognition, high-search topics with massive recent news coverage and an existing documentary/true-crime audience.
Financial-fraud / scam storytelling ('how the con worked', 'where the money went') is one of the highest-performing, most shareable explainer genres.
Younger audiences strongly over-index on crypto content; broadens the channel's reach beyond classic court-case viewers.
competition: Many videos recap the headlines and the courtroom verdict; fewer make the actual MECHANISM of the fraud — the Alameda backdoor and the commingling of customer funds — the clear, visual spine of the story for a general audience.
differentiation: Treats FTX not as 'crypto went down' but as a classic, ancient fraud (spending other people's deposited money) wearing new technology — and makes the hidden mechanism understandable to someone who knows nothing about crypto. The hidden room behind 'your funds are safe'.
research_feasibility: High: US v. Samuel Bankman-Fried (SDNY, 2023) is a public federal criminal case; the indictment, verdict (convicted on all 7 counts, Nov 2 2023) and sentence (25 years, March 2024) are matters of public record; extensive contemporaneous reporting and the bankruptcy filings document the fund flows. Verify every figure against primary/court records before script lock.
visual_feasibility: High, all symbolic: trading-desk and server-room motifs, a glowing 'backdoor' line of code, vaults and flowing coins, Bahamas penthouse silhouette, courthouse exterior (reuse SCOTUS-style stock with NY federal courthouse framing), balance-sheet graphics. No real likeness of any living person; depict figures as anonymous silhouettes only.
risk_notes: RECENT case with a LIVING, named defendant (Sam Bankman-Fried). Highest care: state only adjudicated facts — he was CONVICTED on all 7 counts (Nov 2 2023) and sentenced to 25 years (Mar 28 2024). Distinguish allegation vs. proven; attribute claims to the trial/prosecution where appropriate.
Do NOT depict any real person's likeness; use anonymous silhouettes. No deepfakes of SBF or any executive.

Other real people (co-operators, customers, executives) must be described accurately and neutrally; prefer roles over naming non-defendants where possible; avoid implying crimes by people not convicted.
Financial figures (~$8B shortfall, donation/real-estate amounts) MUST be sourced to court records / official filings before script lock; use ranges and 'prosecutors said / the court found' framing.
No financial advice; add 'not financial or legal advice' disclaimer. Advertiser-safe (no violence/sex); fraud topic is monetization-friendly if factual and neutral.
Generated visuals are symbolic reconstruction, never presented as authentic footage (invariant 11).
kill_conditions: Cannot source the core holding (convicted on all 7 counts of wire fraud / conspiracy, SDNY, Nov 2 2023) and the customer-fund shortfall to court records plus one independent reference.
Cannot tell the fund-flow mechanism accurately without speculating beyond the proven record.
